projects.covert.title: site.hopen
projects.covert.title.text
site.hclose
site.br
site.popen
Prior to 2008 my requirement for virtualisation was quite modest. For one thing, my hardware could not run any significant load.
For another, I really only required it for the purpose of running one or two windows applications, on rare occasions, and for testing
new Slackware distributions prior to deployment or upgrade.
site.pclose
site.br
site.popen
When I took a position with "Cray, Australia", I was unable to work on my customer's site until I had been inducted and, as a consequence,
spent a few months in the office with not set schedule of work. I set about building some clusters of VMs so that I could learn how the PBS
Scheduler works, and I also tried to get some mpich-based cluster applications working. It became apparent, very quickly, that separate scripts
would not 'cut it'. I realised, later, that a static config with a shell or awk script was also not ideal. Thus was born the idea for COVERT, though,
that name did not precipitate until some years later — a tongue in cheek dig at my job role at the time.
site.pclose
site.br
projects.covert.title.text: Chris' Own Virtualisation Enviroment for Research and Testing (COVERT)

projects.covert.keydesign.title: site.br
site.hopen
Design Themes and Philosophy
site.hclose
site.br
site.popen
From its inception, COVERT existed to maintain configuration and to manage the complexity around Qemu command-line arguments. Although it has grown
somewhat organically, recent public events have brought its underlying philosophy into sharp relief. The ongoing, catastrophic data spills across the
health and telco sectors are not accidental anomalies; they are the predictable, structural consequence of modern enterprise IT bloat and 'flowchart admins'.
site.pclose
site.br
site.popen
Modern corporate IT has abandoned foundational engineering in favour of multi-layered, vendor-locked stacks. When you pile commercial hypervisors,
opaque web-auth layers, and unsafe, un-audited SUID root wrappers on top of physical hardware, vulnerability accumulates aggressively at the interfaces.
We see the consequences of this corporate irresponsibility across the landscape — where medical research institutes for example are sold massive,
convoluted enterprise solutions they cannot easily apply to their core business, when the baseline reality is that they just want to execute BLAST or
other bioinformatics workloads deterministically under a Slurm scheduler. COVERT rejects this vendor-driven over-engineering entirely. It enforces strict
goals in pursuit of:
site.pclose
site.br
projects.covert.keydesign.5: Preservation of configuration and operational provenance
projects.covert.keydesign.6: Support for containers, heterogeneous computing environments, and various bootstrapping mechanisms
